title: Perceived importance of national funding for anxiety/depression research vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Share who say it's "extremely important" for the national government to fund research on anxiety/depression. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
note: GDP per capita is expressed in [international-$](#dod:int_dollar_abbreviation) at 2021 prices.
caption:
Perceived importance of national funding for anxiety/depression research vs. GDP per capita

Share who say it's "extremely important" for the national government to fund research on anxiety/depression. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot visualizes the relationship between the share of people who consider national funding for anxiety and depression research "extremely important" and the GDP per capita for various countries in 2020. The vertical axis represents the percentage of respondents, ranging from 20% to 90%, while the horizontal axis displays GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices on a logarithmic scale from $1,000 to $100,000. Each circle represents a country, color-coded by region, with circle size corresponding to population. Countries in Africa, such as Ethiopia, Senegal, and Tanzania, cluster on the left side with GDP per capita below $5,000, yet show high perceived importance, generally between 60% and 70%. In contrast, many European nations like Ireland, Malta, and Poland appear on the right side with GDP per capita exceeding $40,000, also registering high importance levels above 70%. Large population centers like India, Bangladesh, and China are represented by large circles in the lower-middle GDP range, with perceived importance values hovering between 35% and 45%. Brazil and Kosovo sit in the middle of the GDP range near $15,000 to $20,000 but show high importance scores near 80%. The United States and Canada appear in the high GDP range with importance levels around 65% to 70%. The distribution shows no single linear trend, as high importance values are found across both low and high GDP spectrums, while mid-range importance values appear scattered throughout the economic spectrum.
